title: husk — runner fleet
description: husk self-hosted runner fleet: control-plane facts from huskd's own /metrics, and in-guest resource metrics scraped off each runner VM's node_exporter. The two layers join on the `backend` + `slot` labels that huskd's http_sd feed attaches to every guest target. See observability.md.

list.name: window
list.label: Rate window
list.description: Averaging window for rates and histogram quantiles. Widen it when recycles are rare enough that percentiles look like noise.

panels.title: Slots
panels.description: Slots husk currently manages, and how many it wants to have.

panels.title: Ready (idle)
panels.description: Warm slots waiting for a job, against the configured floor. Idle below min_ready for long = the pool cannot keep up.

panels.title: Fleet utilisation
panels.description: Fraction of slots running a job right now.

panels.title: Not healthy
panels.description: Slots that are up but have no runner past the grace period (unhealthy), or that the backend put in ERROR.

panels.title: Reconcile age
panels.description: Seconds since the least recently reconciled pool ticked. huskd can be up and scraping while its loop is wedged — this is the number that catches that.

panels.title: Guest targets down
panels.description: Runner VMs published to Prometheus whose node_exporter is not answering. On libvirt the scrape goes through huskd, so huskd being down shows up here too.

panels.title: Slots by state
panels.description: The stack is actual slots; the dashed lines are what config asks for. A stack that sits below `desired` means creates are failing or the backend is out of capacity.

panels.title: Slot state
panels.description: One lane per slot, coloured by state. Derived from husk_slot_state_seconds_total (see the panel query): a slot caught mid-transition reads as 'transition' for a single interval. Lanes appear and disappear as slots are created and destroyed.

targets.expr:
sum by (backend, slot) (
  (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="idle"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 1
  or (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="busy"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 2
  or (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="starting"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 3
  or (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="needs_recycle"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 4
  or (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="unhealthy"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 5
  or (rate(husk_slot_state_seconds_total{backend=~"$backend",slot=~"$slot",state="error"}[$__rate_interval]) > bool 0.5) * 6
)

panels.title: Where slot time goes
panels.description: Share of total slot-seconds spent in each state over $window. This is the honest capacity number: time in starting/needs_recycle is capacity you paid for and could not sell.

panels.title: Slot churn
panels.description: Recycles are the normal path (rebuild in place after a job). Creates and destroys are the pool changing size — a steady stream of destroys with a reason attached is churn worth explaining.

panels.title: Recycle p50 / p90
panels.description: Rebuild issued → runner online, over $window.

panels.title: Cloud-init p50 / p90
panels.description: Slot ACTIVE → runner online: the part of a recycle husk's own image and cloud-init own. The rest is the backend booting the VM.

panels.title: Recycles in window
panels.description: Sample count behind the quantiles on this row. Small numbers here mean the percentiles are noise.

panels.title: Boot total p90
panels.description: Guest-reported systemd boot time (husk-bootreport), i.e. kernel to userspace-ready — a subset of cloud-init time.

panels.title: Recycle duration
panels.description: Whole bring-up, by pool. Watch this across an image bump — it is the metric that tells you whether the new golden image got slower.

panels.title: Recycle duration distribution
panels.description: Same data, distribution over time — shows a bimodal bring-up (e.g. cache hit vs cache miss) that percentiles average away.

panels.title: Cloud-init duration
panels.description: ACTIVE → runner online. Compare against recycle duration: the gap is backend/hypervisor time, this part is husk's.

panels.title: Guest boot phases (p90)
panels.description: kernel / initrd / userspace / total, off the husk-bootreport block on the serial console. Isolates a slow boot to the layer that caused it.

panels.title: Last recycle, per slot
panels.description: Most recent bring-up per slot. One slot far above the others usually means a sick hypervisor, not a sick image.

panels.title: Recycles per slot
panels.description: Cycle counter per slot — how many jobs each slot has served since it was created. A slot whose cycle stops advancing while others climb is stuck.

panels.title: Fail-safed ticks
panels.description: Ticks that bailed out before mutating anything, over $window. This is the single most important failure: huskd looks healthy but has stopped acting on reality.

panels.title: GitHub poll failure rate
panels.description: Share of runner-listing polls that failed. Failures keep the last snapshot; sustained failure makes it stale, which then shows up as stale_runner_snapshot aborts.

panels.title: Guest scrape failures
panels.description: libvirt guest metric scrapes bridged through huskd that failed. Only affects observability, not the fleet.

panels.title: Tick p95
panels.description: Wall-clock of one reconcile tick. Creeping up = a backend call is getting slow.

panels.title: Ticks/min
panels.description: Should track the configured interval. A drop is the loop stalling.

panels.title: Reconcile aborts by reason
panels.description: list_slots = the backend would not answer. no_runner_snapshot / stale_runner_snapshot = GitHub did. Different fixes, hence the split.

panels.title: Action failures by action
panels.description: Per-hour rate of non-fatal failed actions (rebuild/create/destroy/delete_runner/…). Cross-reference with the slot state plot: a burst of failed rebuilds shows up there as slots stuck in needs_recycle.

panels.title: Image bytes
panels.description: Total qcow2 bytes husk is responsible for, across cache and hosts. Deduped by (host, kind) before exposition, so summing is safe here.

panels.title: Fullest volume
panels.description: Headroom is what predicts the outage: husk's own footprint can be flat while the volume fills for other reasons.

panels.title: Least free space
panels.description: Absolute free bytes on the tightest filesystem. Compare against one golden image (~10-40 GiB): below that, the next pull fails.

panels.title: Image bytes by location
panels.description: kind=cache is huskd's own pull cache; other kinds are per-host image stores. Growth without a matching count increase means images got bigger, not more numerous.

panels.title: Filesystem headroom
panels.description: Two kinds sharing one disk report identical numbers — these describe the filesystem, not the directory. Aggregate with min()/max(), never sum().

panels.title: Fleet CPU
panels.description: 100% = every vCPU in the fleet is executing. Denominator is the count of vCPUs currently reporting, so it tracks the fleet as it resizes.

panels.title: vCPUs online
panels.description: Total vCPUs across reporting runner VMs — the denominator of Fleet CPU.

panels.title: Hottest VM disk
panels.description: Fullest filesystem on any runner VM. Filesystem fill is the reason node_exporter has to live in the guest — the hypervisor cannot see it.

panels.title: VMs reporting
panels.description: Runner VMs currently scraped. Only runner-online slots are published to Prometheus, so this tracks idle+busy, not the whole pool.

panels.title: Fleet CPU usage
panels.description: 100% means every vCPU in the fleet is firing. Sustained low values while slots are busy means jobs are not CPU-bound — check iowait before buying more cores.

panels.title: Per-VM CPU
panels.description: Per-VM CPU utilisation, 100% = all of that VM's vCPUs busy.

panels.title: Per-VM memory used
panels.description: MemAvailable-based, so page cache does not read as pressure.

panels.title: Per-VM disk used (fullest mount)
panels.description: The fullest real filesystem on each VM. A job that fills the disk fails in confusing ways; this is the early warning.

panels.title: Per-VM CPU distribution
panels.description: Are VMs uniformly loaded, or is one pegged while the rest idle? Bands here mean the fleet is unbalanced.

panels.title: Per-VM load, normalised
panels.description: 1-minute load average divided by that VM's vCPU count. >1 sustained = the VM is oversubscribed, which CPU% alone will not show once it saturates.

panels.title: Per-VM disk I/O
panels.description: Builds that look CPU-idle are often here instead.

panels.title: Per-VM network
panels.description: Dominated by artifact/cache traffic; a slot stuck at zero rx during a job is usually a wedged runner.

panels.title: Slot inventory
panels.description: The husk_slot_info join table — what a slot's `slot` label resolves to in the real world (IP, hypervisor, GitHub runner name), plus its cycle count. This is the panel to start from when a guest metric looks wrong and you need to go SSH somewhere.
